selection_policy.goal: Identify non-memory-corruption CVEs that may still affect a Rust reimplementation of libvips.
included_when: The CVE is a direct libvips issue and the root cause is still possible in Rust through concurrency, arithmetic, parser logic, or data-initialization mistakes.
The CVE is not primarily a heap/stack memory-corruption bug such as use-after-free, out-of-bounds read/write, or heap buffer overflow.

A conditional inclusion is allowed when safe Rust removes the exact C failure mode but the same bug class could return if the port uses unsafe buffer management, FFI-backed buffers, or ports the same custom parser logic.
excluded_when: The issue is primarily memory corruption or other C-specific pointer misuse with no clear language-agnostic failure mode.
The issue is in a third-party dependency or ecosystem wrapper rather than libvips itself.
The only available evidence is a null-dereference symptom with no stronger indication that the underlying defect survives an idiomatic Rust design.

relevant_cves.description: In libvips before 8.6.3, a NULL function pointer dereference vulnerability was found in the vips_region_generate function in region.c, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted image file. This occurs because of a race condition involving a failed delayed load and other worker threads.
relevant_cves.rust_port_relevance: The root cause is a race between delayed-load failure and worker threads. Safe Rust removes the NULL-function-pointer symptom, but a Rust port can still mishandle shared error state and turn the same condition into a panic, incorrect scheduling, or denial of service.

relevant_cves.description: libvips before 8.7.4 generates output images from uninitialized memory locations when processing corrupted input image data because iofuncs/memory.c does not zero out allocated memory. This can result in leaking raw process memory contents through the output image.
relevant_cves.rust_port_relevance: Fully safe Rust would not expose uninitialized bytes, but a high-performance image pipeline may still use unsafe allocation patterns, partially initialized buffers, or FFI-owned memory. The port should treat full output-buffer initialization as a hard invariant, especially on error paths.

relevant_cves.description: Division-By-Zero vulnerability in Libvips 8.10.5 in the function vips_eye_point, eye.c#L83, and function vips_mask_point, mask.c#L85.
relevant_cves.rust_port_relevance: Rust does not prevent divide-by-zero in release builds from becoming a runtime failure. Any port of the geometry or mask-generation code still needs explicit zero and degenerate-dimension checks.

relevant_cves.relevance_category: malformed text handling and parser boundary checks
relevant_cves.description: libvips is a demand-driven, horizontally threaded image processing library. A specially crafted SVG input can cause libvips versions 8.14.3 or earlier to segfault when attempting to parse a malformed UTF-8 character. Users should upgrade to libvips version 8.14.4 (or later) when processing untrusted input.
relevant_cves.rust_port_relevance: Idiomatic Rust string APIs remove the exact NULL-dereference failure mode, but malformed UTF-8 and truncated multibyte sequences are still untrusted-input cases the port must handle explicitly. This stays relevant if the Rust port keeps custom byte-level SVG or text parsing instead of delegating to a mature parser.

relevant_cves.relevance_category: matrix header parsing and input-size validation
relevant_cves.description: A vulnerability has been found in libvips up to 8.18.0. The impacted element is the function vips_foreign_load_matrix_header of the file libvips/foreign/matrixload.c. The manipulation leads to null pointer dereference. The attack needs to be performed locally. The identifier of the patch is d4ce337c76bff1b278d7085c3c4f4725e3aa6ece. To fix this issue, it is recommended to deploy a patch.
relevant_cves.rust_port_relevance: Although reported as a null-pointer dereference, the upstream fix adds guards for empty and very large matrix inputs in matrixload. That makes this a language-agnostic parser and bounds-validation issue: a Rust port still needs to reject empty headers and cap unreasonable dimensions or counts before allocation, iteration, or indexing.

relevant_cves.description: A vulnerability was found in libvips 8.19.0. Impacted is the function vips_extract_area_build of the file libvips/conversion/extract.c. The manipulation of the argument extract_area results in integer overflow. The attack requires a local approach. The exploit has been made public and could be used. The patch is identified as 24795bb3d19d84f7b6f5ed86451ad556c8f2fe70. It is advisable to implement a patch to correct this issue.
relevant_cves.rust_port_relevance: This is a language-agnostic bug class. Rust debug builds trap many integer overflows, but release builds wrap by default for primitive integers. Coordinate arithmetic and size checks in a Rust port still need checked addition, range validation, and careful signed/unsigned conversions before slicing or allocating.

reviewed_but_excluded.reason: Non-memory-corruption, but the available evidence is an uninitialized variable in deprecated code. The exact failure mode depends on C-style uninitialized locals and is not a strong language-agnostic risk for an idiomatic Rust port.
Command injection in the Ruby image_processing wrapper, not a libvips library flaw.
